hero.title: Scroll that feels
hero.subtitle: SmoothScroll makes every mouse wheel movement feel silky — like you're using a premium trackpad. No learning curve. No bloat. Just smooth.

points.title: Jerky, stuttering motion
points.description: Every scroll tick lurches. Your hand expects smooth — your screen delivers chop.

trayPreview.title: It lives in your system tray
trayPreview.subtitle: One click from the tray opens a compact panel with everything you need — toggle smoothing on or off, decide whether SmoothScroll launches with Windows, jump straight into per-app exclusions, or open the full settings when you want to fine-tune the physics. No window cluttering your desktop, no taskbar entry stealing focus. It stays out of the way until the moment you reach for it.

points: Source-available (FSL-1.1 → Apache 2.0) — audit the code yourself
No ads, no premium tier, no upsell
Built in the open on GitHub

windows.label: Windows
steps: Run the .exe — no install wizard, no admin required

macos.label: macOS
steps: Grant Accessibility permission in System Settings → Privacy → Accessibility

note.macos: Accessibility permission is required for global input interception

questions.q: Does this work with all apps?
questions.a: SmoothScroll intercepts the Windows mouse wheel input before it reaches any application, so it works with virtually everything — Chrome, VS Code, File Explorer, Adobe apps, and more.
No. SmoothScroll runs at near-zero CPU when idle and only processes input during scroll events. Most users see no measurable battery impact.

questions.q: Is it safe? What does it install?
questions.a: SmoothScroll is a standalone executable. No service, no daemon, no system driver. It doesn't install any network listeners or telemetry. You can verify this by reading the source on GitHub (FSL-1.1-Apache-2.0).

questions.q: How is this different from Windows' built-in smooth scrolling?
questions.a: Windows' built-in smooth scroll is app-specific and inconsistent. SmoothScroll provides a unified, configurable curve applied globally — with per-app profiles, adjustable strength, and instant toggle.

questions.q: Does it work with gaming mice or high-DPI devices?
questions.a: Yes. SmoothScroll respects the actual scroll delta from your hardware and applies smoothing on top. Works with all mice tested so far, including high-refresh and gaming mice.

questions.q: Can I disable it for specific apps?
questions.a: Yes. Per-app exclusion profiles are built in. Just open the tray panel and add apps to the exclude list.
Windows 10 and Windows 11 are fully supported.
Just delete the executable. No uninstaller needed — there is nothing else on your system.

seo.title: How SmoothScroll Works — Every Setting Explained
seo.description: Complete walkthrough of every SmoothScroll feature, setting, and keyboard shortcut. Tune scroll feel for reading, coding, design, and gaming.

hero.title: See how SmoothScroll
hero.subtitle: A page-by-page tour of every tab, every setting, and every keyboard shortcut — so you can dial in the exact scroll feel you want.

demo.caption: Every tab, every setting — recorded in real time
demo.alt: Animated walkthrough of the SmoothScroll settings window: Scroll, Devices, Advanced, Apps, Game mode, Behavior, About
demo.hint: Tip: each tab gets its own tour below.

bigPicture.title: The big picture
bigPicture.subtitle: Three steps run for every wheel tick — 120 times a second.

steps.title: 1. Intercept
steps.description: A low-level hook (Windows WH_MOUSE_LL / macOS CGEventTap) catches each raw wheel event before any app sees it.

steps.title: 2. Smooth
steps.description: An eased curve — Exponential, Cubic, Quintic, or Linear — interpolates the delta over your animation window. Acceleration stacks momentum if you scroll quickly.

steps.title: 3. Emit
steps.description: Synthetic wheel events fire at 120 Hz to the foreground app. Excluded apps and games skip the engine and pass through untouched.

tabs.title: Every tab in the app
tabs.subtitle: Open Settings (left-click the tray icon) to follow along. The order below matches the sidebar.

sections.label: Scroll
sections.intro: The main feel controls. Most users only ever touch this tab.

settings.name: Enable
settings.what: Master toggle. Off = native OS scroll, on = SmoothScroll engine.
settings.why: Quick A/B without uninstalling. Also bound to your global hotkey.
settings.tip: Use the global hotkey (Ctrl+Alt+S) to flip this without leaving your keyboard.

settings.name: Step size
settings.what: Pixels scrolled per wheel notch.
settings.why: Bigger = covers more page per click; smaller = finer control.
settings.tip: Drop to 96 for code review, raise to 200 for long PDFs.

settings.name: Animation time
settings.what: How long each scroll movement takes to settle.
settings.why: Longer = silkier glide; shorter = snappier response.
settings.tip: Anything under 120 ms feels almost native; over 300 ms feels mac-trackpad-y.

settings.name: Smooth easing
settings.what: Apply an easing curve to each movement. Off = linear ramp.
settings.why: Easing is what makes scroll feel natural instead of robotic.

settings.name: Easing curve
settings.what: Four shapes — Exponential Out, Cubic Out, Quintic Out, Linear.
settings.why: Each curve decelerates differently. Quintic feels mac-like; Linear feels purely mechanical.
settings.tip: Try Quintic if you want the longest, smoothest tail.

settings.name: Reverse direction
settings.what: Inverts wheel direction (natural-style, like macOS).
settings.why: Match a Mac you also use, or just preference.

settings.name: Smooth horizontal scrolling
settings.what: Apply smoothing to tilt-wheel and Shift+wheel scrolls.
settings.why: Wide spreadsheets, timelines, image strips.

settings.name: Shift converts to horizontal
settings.what: Holding Shift while scrolling becomes horizontal scroll.
settings.why: Useful when your mouse has no tilt-wheel.

sections.label: Devices
sections.intro: Smoothing for keyboard scrolling and precision touchpads — separate from mouse-wheel handling.

settings.name: Enable keyboard smoothing (Windows only)
settings.what: Apply easing to PageUp/Down and arrow-key scrolling.
settings.why: Same flow benefits as wheel smoothing, but for keyboard navigation.
settings.tip: Enable 'Skip in text inputs' so arrow keys still work normally inside text fields.

settings.name: PageUp / PageDown step
settings.what: How many wheel-equivalent notches a single PageUp/PageDown emits.
settings.why: Tune jump distance for long documents.

settings.name: Arrow step
settings.what: How many notches each arrow keypress emits.
settings.why: Smaller = more precise navigation.

settings.name: Smooth touchpad scrolling
settings.what: Apply easing to high-resolution precision touchpad input.
settings.why: Touchpads emit pixel-level deltas; this smooths them too.

settings.name: Pixel multiplier
settings.what: Scale raw touchpad pixel deltas before smoothing.
settings.why: Match touchpad sensitivity to your mouse wheel.

settings.name: Acceleration factor
settings.what: Boost large two-finger flicks. 0 disables acceleration entirely.
settings.why: Lets quick flicks travel farther while small drags stay precise.

sections.label: Advanced
sections.intro: The deep-tuning pane. Touch when defaults don't quite match your hand.

settings.name: Acceleration window
settings.what: Time window during which consecutive notches stack momentum.
settings.why: Lets quick flicks scroll farther without faster ticks.
settings.tip: Higher = more momentum buildup. Set to 0 to disable acceleration.

settings.name: Max acceleration
settings.what: Hard cap on the acceleration multiplier.
settings.why: Prevents runaway speed during fast spinning.

settings.name: Tail to head ratio
settings.what: How sharp the deceleration curve is.
settings.why: Higher = quicker initial speed, longer slow tail.

settings.name: Pass-through Ctrl + wheel
settings.what: When Ctrl is held, send raw wheel ticks (no smoothing).
settings.why: Photoshop, Figma, browsers, VS Code use Ctrl+wheel for zoom — smoothing would lag the zoom.

settings.name: Pass-through Alt + wheel
settings.what: When Alt is held, send raw wheel ticks.
settings.why: Premiere, DAWs, and timeline editors zoom or scrub with Alt+wheel.

settings.name: Clear inertia on modifier
settings.what: Stop pending smooth scroll the instant Ctrl/Alt is pressed.
settings.why: Makes zoom feel instant after a smooth scroll.

settings.name: Edge auto-scroll
settings.what: Auto-scroll when the cursor enters a screen-edge zone.
settings.why: Hands-free scrolling when you're already drag-and-dropping.
settings.range: On / Off + zone size + max speed
settings.tip: Pair with a modifier requirement to avoid accidental triggers.

sections.label: Apps
sections.intro: Per-application overrides. Different apps deserve different feel.

settings.name: Scroll profiles
settings.what: Named presets — e.g. Mac-like, Snappy, Slow Reading — each with its own step size, animation time, and easing.
settings.why: Avoid retuning the global slider every time you switch tasks.
settings.tip: Build profiles around how an app is used, not what it is. 'Long-form reading' applies to PDFs, books, and articles alike.

settings.name: Per-app assignments
settings.what: Map a process name (e.g. chrome.exe) to a specific profile.
settings.why: Chrome can glide while VS Code stays snappy.

settings.name: Excluded apps
settings.what: Apps that bypass SmoothScroll entirely.
settings.why: Some apps (anti-cheat, specialty tools) prefer raw wheel input.

sections.label: Game mode
sections.intro: Auto-disable smoothing whenever a known game is in the foreground.

settings.name: Auto-disable in games
settings.what: Pauses the engine while any process from the games list is focused.
settings.why: Games need raw scroll input — for weapon switching, zoom scopes, etc.

settings.name: Known games list
settings.what: Editable list of process names that trigger Game mode.
settings.why: Add your own games; remove false positives.

sections.label: Behavior
sections.intro: How the app itself behaves on launch, in the tray, and when you press the hotkey.

settings.name: Global hotkey
settings.what: System-wide shortcut to toggle smoothing on/off.
settings.why: Flip without alt-tabbing into Settings.

settings.name: Hotkey combination
settings.what: Click the field, then press the keys you want.
settings.why: Avoid clashing with other apps you use.

settings.name: Start with Windows / macOS
settings.what: Launches SmoothScroll on login.
settings.why: Set-and-forget — smooth scroll from the first second of every session.

settings.name: Start minimized to tray
settings.what: Skip the settings window on launch.
settings.why: Pair with Start with Windows for a fully background experience.

settings.name: Show tray icon state
settings.what: Tray icon visually flips between ON and OFF.
settings.why: Glanceable confirmation that smoothing is active.

settings.name: Respect system Reduce Motion
settings.what: Three modes — Auto (follow OS), Always instant, Always smooth.
settings.why: Honor accessibility preferences automatically.

sections.label: About
sections.intro: Version, updates, backup, and local stats — the housekeeping tab.

settings.name: Version & updates
settings.what: Check for and install signed updates.
settings.why: Stay on the latest fixes without hunting for downloads.

settings.name: Backup & restore
settings.what: Export your full settings as JSON, import on another machine.
settings.why: Move presets between PCs without redoing every slider.

settings.name: Local stats
settings.what: Sessions and total usage time, computed on-device only.
settings.why: See how much you've used SmoothScroll. Nothing is uploaded.

settings.name: Open log folder
settings.what: Opens %APPDATA%\SmoothScroll\logs (or ~/Library/Logs/SmoothScroll on macOS).
settings.why: Useful when filing a bug report.

settings.name: Re-run setup wizard
settings.what: Reopens the 3-step onboarding.
settings.why: Reset to a known-good preset if you've over-tweaked.

shortcuts.title: Keyboard shortcuts
shortcuts.subtitle: Press these from anywhere on your system, or only inside the settings window — the Scope column tells you which.

rows.action: Open quick menu (Enable, Settings, Logs, Quit)

shortcuts.hotkeyNote: The global hotkey is rebindable in Behavior → Hotkey combination. Pick something that doesn't clash with your other tools.

tray.title: The system tray
tray.subtitle: SmoothScroll has no taskbar window — everything lives in the tray.

rightClick.title: Right-click the tray icon
rightClick.description: Native OS context menu (faster on slower machines).

perApp.title: Current app card
perApp.description: The tray panel shows the foreground app and its category. Toggle smoothing for that app, or assign a profile — all without opening full settings.

recipes.title: Pro tips & recipes
recipes.subtitle: Battle-tested presets for common workflows. Open Apps → Profiles to save them.

items.name: Long-form reading
items.scenario: PDFs, articles, books — skim or savor without losing your place.

items.name: Coding in VS Code / JetBrains
items.scenario: Navigate code without overshooting and losing your line.

items.name: Photoshop / Figma / Illustrator
items.scenario: Frequent Ctrl+wheel zoom; smooth scroll within layer panels.
settings: Advanced → Clear inertia on modifier: ON

items.name: Premiere / DAWs
settings: Apps → assign Snappy profile to your editor

items.name: Gaming
items.scenario: Weapon wheel, zoom scope — anything that reads raw notches.
settings: Game mode → Auto-disable in games: ON
Add your game's process to the list (e.g. csgo.exe)

items.name: Laptop on battery
items.scenario: Squeeze every minute — reduce CPU work.

privacy.title: What it does (and doesn't) do
points: Reads mouse-wheel events at the OS hook layer — same API as accessibility tools and screen readers.
Does not inject into other processes.
Does not install drivers, services, or daemons.
Does not make network calls except checking GitHub for new releases (toggleable).
Logs rotate daily, retained 7 days, stay on your device.
Source-available under FSL-1.1 → auto Apache 2.0 after 2 years.

finalCta.title: Ready to feel the difference?
finalCta.subtitle: Install in 60 seconds. Tune in 60 more. Then forget it's even there.
